item.title: Monotone Data Flow Analysis Frameworks
item.description: Monotone Data Flow Analysis Frameworks Performing compile time optimization requires solving a class of problems, called Global data flow analysis problems (abbreviated as gdfap...
item.content:encoded:
Performing compile time optimization requires solving a class of problems, called Global data flow analysis problems (abbreviated as gdfap's), involving determination of information which is distributed throughout the program.

Monotone data flow analysis frameworks are used to analyze the flow of information through a program. They are based on the idea of propagating data flow information through the control flow graph (CFG) of the program. The analysis is called "monotone" because the information gathered at each point in the program is monotonically increasing(or decreasing) as the analysis progresses.

Lattice Theoretic

Definition: A flow graph is a triple G=(N,E,n0)G = (N, E, n_0)G=(N,E,n0​), where:

  1. NNN is a finite set of nodes.
  2. EEE is a subset of N×NN \times NN×N called the edges. The edge (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) enters node y and leaves node xxx. We say that xxx is a predecessor of yyy, and yyy a successor of xxx.
  3. n0n_0n0​ in NNN is the initial node. There is a path from n0n_0n0​ to every node.

Definition: A semilattice is a set LLL with a binary meet operation ∧\wedge∧ such that for all a,b,c∈La, b, c \in La,b,c∈L:

a∧a=aidempotenta∧b=b∧acommutativea∧(b∧c)=(a∧b)∧caccociative \begin{align*} & a \wedge a = a & \text{idempotent} \\ & a \wedge b = b \wedge a & \text{commutative} \\ & a \wedge ( b \wedge c ) = (a \wedge b ) \wedge c & \text{accociative} \end{align*} ​a∧a=aa∧b=b∧aa∧(b∧c)=(a∧b)∧c​idempotentcommutativeaccociative​

Definition: Given a semilattice LLL and elements, a,b∈La, b \in La,b∈L, we say that

a≥b  ⟺  a∧b=ba>b  ⟺  a∧b=b ∧ a≠b \begin{aligned} & a \geq b \quad \iff \quad a \wedge b = b \\ & a > b \quad \iff \quad a \wedge b = b \ \land \ a \neq b \end{aligned} ​a≥b⟺a∧b=ba>b⟺a∧b=b ∧ a=b​

also a≤ba \leq ba≤b means b≥ab \geq ab≥a and a<ba < ba<b means b>ab > ab>a. We extend the notation of the meet operation to arbitrary finite sets by saying

⋀1≤i≤nxi=x1∧x2∧...∧xn \bigwedge\limits_{1 \leq i \leq n} x_i = x_1 \wedge x_2 \wedge ... \wedge x_n 1≤i≤n⋀​xi​=x1​∧x2​∧...∧xn​

Definition: A semilattice LLL is said to have a bottom element ⊥\bot⊥, if for all $ x \in L, \bot \wedge x = \bot$. LLL is said to have a top element ⊤\top⊤, if ⊤∧x=x\top \wedge x = x⊤∧x=x for all x∈Lx \in Lx∈L. We assume from here on that every semilattice has a bottom element, but not necessarily a top element.

Definition: Given a semilattice LLL, a sequence of elements x1,x2,...,xnx_1, x_2, ..., x_nx1​,x2​,...,xn​ in LLL forms a chain if $ x_i > x_{i-1}$ for 1≤i<n1 \leq i < n1≤i<n. LLL is said to be bounded if for each x∈Lx \in Lx∈L there is a constant bxb_xbx​ such that each chain beginning with xxx has length at most bxb_xbx​.

If LLL is bounded, then we can take meets over countably infinite sets if we define ⋀x∈Sx\bigwedge\limits_{x\in S}xx∈S⋀​x , where S={x1,x2,...}S = \{ x_1, x_2, ...\}S={x1​,x2​,...} , to be lim⁡n→∞⋀1≤i≤nxi\lim_{n \to\infty} \bigwedge\limits_{1 \leq i \leq n} x_ilimn→∞​1≤i≤n⋀​xi​ . The fact that LLL is bounded assures us there is an integer mmm such that ⋀x∈Sx=⋀1≤i≤mxi\bigwedge\limits_{x\in S} x = \bigwedge\limits_{1 \leq i \leq m} x_ix∈S⋀​x=1≤i≤m⋀​xi​ .

Partial Orders and Greatest Lower Bounds

As we shall see, the meet operator of a semilattice define a partial order on the values of the domain. A relation ≤\leq≤ is a partial  orderpartial\;orderpartialorder on a set VVV if ∀x,y,z∈V\forall x, y, z \in V∀x,y,z∈V :

  1. x≤xx \leq xx≤x (the partial order is reflexivereflexivereflexive).
  2. If x≤yx \leq yx≤y and y≤xy \leq xy≤x , then x=yx = yx=y (the partial order is antisymmeticeantisymmeticeantisymmetice).
  3. If x≤yx \leq yx≤y and y≤zy \leq zy≤z , then x≤zx \leq zx≤z (the partial order is transitivetransitivetransitive).

The pair (V,≤)(V, \leq)(V,≤) is called a posetposetposet, or partially  ordered  setpartially\;ordered\;setpartiallyorderedset. It is also convenient to have a <<< relation for a post, defined as

x<y  ⟺  (x≤y)  and  (x≠y). x < y \quad \iff (x \leq y)\;\text{and}\;(x \neq y). x<y⟺(x≤y)and(x=y).

Suppose (V,∧)(V, \wedge)(V,∧) is a semilattice. A greatest  lower  boundgreatest \; lower \; boundgreatestlowerbound (or glbglbglb) of domain elements xxx and yyy is an element ggg such that

  1. g≤xg \leq xg≤x,
  2. g≤yg \leq yg≤y, and
  3. If zzz is any element such that z≤x  and  z≤y,  then  z≤gz \leq x \; \text{and} \; z \leq y, \; \text{then} \; z \leq gz≤xandz≤y,thenz≤g.

It turns out that the meet of xxx and yyy is their only greatest lower bound. To see why, let g=x∧yg = x \wedge yg=x∧y. Observe that:

  • g≤xg \leq xg≤x because (x∧y)∧x=x∧y(x \wedge y) \wedge x = x \wedge y(x∧y)∧x=x∧y.
  • g≤yg \leq yg≤y by a similar argument.
  • Suppose zzz is any element such that z≤xz \leq xz≤x and z≤yz \leq yz≤y. We claim z≤gz \leq gz≤g, and therefore, zzz cannot be a glb of x and y unless it is also ggg. In proof: (z∧g)=(z∧(x∧y))=((z∧x)∧y)(z \wedge g) = (z \wedge (x \wedge y)) = ((z \wedge x) \wedge y)(z∧g)=(z∧(x∧y))=((z∧x)∧y). Since z≤xz \leq xz≤x, we know (z∧x)=z(z \wedge x) = z(z∧x)=z , so (z∧g)=(z∧y)(z \wedge g) = ( z \wedge y)(z∧g)=(z∧y). Since z≤yz \leq yz≤y , we know z∧y=zz \wedge y = zz∧y=z , and therefore z∧g=zz \wedge g = zz∧g=z. We have proven z≤gz \leq gz≤g and conclude g=x∧yg = x \wedge yg=x∧y is the only glb of xxx and yyy.

Monotone Data Flow Analysis Frameworks

Definition: Given a bounded semilattice LLL, a set of functions FFF on LLL is said to be a monotone  function  space  associated  with  Lmonotone \; function \; space \; associated \; with \; LmonotonefunctionspaceassociatedwithL if the following conditions are satisfied:

  • [M1] Each f∈Ff\in Ff∈F satisfies the monotoncitymonotoncitymonotoncity condition,

    (∀x,y∈L)(∀f∈F)  [f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)] (\forall x, y \in L)(\forall f \in F)\;[f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y)] (∀x,y∈L)(∀f∈F)[f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)]

  • [M2] There exists an identify function iii in FFF, such that

    (∀x∈L)  [i(x)=x]. (\forall x \in L)\; [i(x) = x]. (∀x∈L)[i(x)=x].

  • [M3] FFF is closed under composition, i.e. f,g∈F⇒f∘g∈Ff, g \in F \Rightarrow f \circ g\in Ff,g∈F⇒f∘g∈F, where

    (∀x,y∈L)  [f∘g(x)=f(g(x))] (\forall x, y \in L) \; [f \circ g (x) = f(g(x))] (∀x,y∈L)[f∘g(x)=f(g(x))]

  • [M4] LLL is equal to the closure of {0}\{ 0 \}{0} under the meet operation and application of functions in FFF.

Observation  1Observation \; 1Observation1. Given a semilattice LLL, let fff be a function on LLL, then

(∀x,y∈L)  [f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)]  ⟺  (∀x,y∈L)  [x≤y  implies  f(x)≤f(y)] (\forall x, y \in L) \; [f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y)] \iff (\forall x, y \in L) \; [x\leq y \; implies \; f(x) \leq f(y)] (∀x,y∈L)[f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)]⟺(∀x,y∈L)[x≤yimpliesf(x)≤f(y)]

proofproofproof. We shall assume (∀x,y∈L)  [x≤y  implies  f(x)≤f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [x\leq y \; implies \; f(x) \leq f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[x≤yimpliesf(x)≤f(y)] and show that (∀x,y∈L)  [f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)] holds. Since x∧yx \wedge yx∧y is the greatest lower bound of xxx and yyy, we know that

x∧y≤xandx∧y≤y x \wedge y \leq x \quad and \quad x \wedge y \leq y x∧y≤xandx∧y≤y

Thus, by (∀x,y∈L)  [x≤y  implies  f(x)≤f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [x\leq y \; implies \; f(x) \leq f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[x≤yimpliesf(x)≤f(y)],

f(x∧y)≤f(x)andf(x∧y)≤f(y). f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \quad and \quad f(x \wedge y) \leq f(y). f(x∧y)≤f(x)andf(x∧y)≤f(y).

Since f(x)∧f(y)f(x) \wedge f(y)f(x)∧f(y) is the greatest lower bound of f(x)f(x)f(x) and f(y)f(y)f(y), we have (∀x,y∈L)  [f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)].

Conversely, let us assume (∀x,y∈L)  [f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)] to prove (∀x,y∈L)  [x≤y  implies  f(x)≤f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [x\leq y \; implies \; f(x) \leq f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[x≤yimpliesf(x)≤f(y)]. We suppose x≤yx \leq yx≤y and use (∀x,y∈L)  [f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [f(x \wedge y) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[f(x∧y)≤f(x)∧f(y)] to conclude f(x)≤f(y)f(x) \leq f(y)f(x)≤f(y). Since x≤yx \leq yx≤y is assumed, x∧y=xx \wedge y = xx∧y=x , by definition. We know that

f(x)≤f(x)∧f(y). f(x) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y). f(x)≤f(x)∧f(y).

Since f(x)∧f(y)f(x) \wedge f(y)f(x)∧f(y) is the glbglbglb of f(x)f(x)f(x) and f(y)f(y)f(y), we know f(x)∧f(y)≤f(y)f(x) \wedge f(y) \leq f(y)f(x)∧f(y)≤f(y). Thus

f(x)≤f(x)∧f(y)≤f(y) f(x) \leq f(x) \wedge f(y) \leq f(y) f(x)≤f(x)∧f(y)≤f(y)

implies (∀x,y∈L)  [x≤y  implies  f(x)≤f(y)](\forall x, y \in L) \; [x\leq y \; implies \; f(x) \leq f(y)](∀x,y∈L)[x≤yimpliesf(x)≤f(y)].

□

Observation  2Observation\;2Observation2. For any bounded semilattice LLL and any countable set S⊆LS \subseteq LS⊆L, if for all x∈Sx \in Sx∈S we have x≥yx \geq yx≥y , then ⋀x∈Sx≥y\bigwedge\limits_{x\in S} x\geq yx∈S⋀​x≥y.

proofproofproof. Consider the meet of all elements in SSS:

m=⋀x∈Sx m = \bigwedge\limits_{x \in S} x m=x∈S⋀​x

The meet mmm is the greatest lower bound of all elements in SSS. By definition, m≤xm \leq xm≤x for all x∈Sx \in Sx∈S. If x≥yx \geq yx≥y for all x∈Sx \in Sx∈S, then every element in SSS is an upper bound for yyy. mmm, being the greatest lower bound of all x∈Sx \in Sx∈S, must also be a lower bound for yyy. Therefore, y∈my \in my∈m. Hence, m=⋀x∈Sx≥ym = \bigwedge\limits_{x \in S} x \geq ym=x∈S⋀​x≥y.

□

Definition: A Monotone data flow analysis framework is a triple D=(L,∧,F)D = (L, \wedge, F)D=(L,∧,F), where

  1. LLL is a bounded semilattice with meet ∧\wedge∧.
  2. FFF is a monotone function space associated with LLL.

A particular instance of a monotone data flow analysis framework is a pair I=(G,M)I = (G, M)I=(G,M), where

  1. G=(N,E,n0)G = (N, E, n_0)G=(N,E,n0​) is a flow graph.
  2. M:N→FM: N \rightarrow FM:N→F is a function which maps each node in NNN to a function in FFF.

Some monotone data flow analysis frameworks satisfy the condition:

(∀x,y∈L)(∀f∈F)  [f(x∧y)=f(x)∧f(y)](distributivity) (\forall x, y \in L)(\forall f \in F)\;[f(x \wedge y) = f(x) \wedge f(y)]\qquad\text{(distributivity)} (∀x,y∈L)(∀f∈F)[f(x∧y)=f(x)∧f(y)](distributivity)

That is, each fff in FFF is a homomorphism on LLL. There are many interesting gdfap's which are monotone data flow analysis frameworks but which do not satisfy the distributivity property. The following are some examples.

Constant Propagation can be formalized as a monotone data flow analysis framework CONST=(L,∧,F)CONST = (L, \wedge, F)CONST=(L,∧,F). Here L⊂2V×RL \subset 2^{V \times R}L⊂2V×R, where V={A1,A2,...}V = \{ A_1, A_2, ...\}V={A1​,A2​,...} is an infinite set of variables and RRR is the set of all real numbers.

  • LLL is the set of functions from finite subsets of VVV to RRR.

  • θ∈L\theta \in Lθ∈L is the function which is undefined for all Ai∈VA_i \in VAi​∈V.

  • The meet operation on LLL is set intersection.

Intuitively, z∈Lz\in Lz∈L stands for the information about variables which we may assume at certain points of the program flow graph. (A,r)∈z(A, r) \in z(A,r)∈z implies the variable A has value rrr.

We define a notation for functions in FFF based on the sequence of assignments whose effect they are to model.

  1. There are functions denoted ⟨A:=B θ C⟩\langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle⟨A:=BθC⟩ and ⟨A:=r⟩\langle A := r \rangle⟨A:=r⟩ in FFF, for each AAA, BBB and CCC in VVV, r∈Rr \in Rr∈R and θ∈{+,−,∗,/}\theta \in \{ +, -, *, / \}θ∈{+,−,∗,/}.

Let z∈Lz \in Lz∈L. Then

  • ⟨A:=B θ C⟩(z)=z′\langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle(z) = z'⟨A:=BθC⟩(z)=z′, where z′(X)=z(X)z'(X) = z(X)z′(X)=z(X) for all X∈V−{A}X \in V - \{A\}X∈V−{A}; z′(A)z'(A)z′(A) is undefined unless z(B)=bz(B) = bz(B)=b and z(C)=cz(C) = cz(C)=c for some bbb and ccc in R, in which case z′(A)=b θ cz'(A) = b\,\theta\,cz′(A)=bθc.

    Assume that zzz represents the semilattice before that the program execution flow enters function ⟨A:=B θ C⟩\langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle⟨A:=BθC⟩, and z′z'z′ the semilattice of the program execution flow after executing ⟨A:=B θ C⟩\langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle⟨A:=BθC⟩. Since ⟨A:=B θ C⟩\langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle⟨A:=BθC⟩ is an assignment statement, so no matter what the variable AAA changes, we always know all other variables will not be affected because the only variable affected is AAA. Hence we can obtain z′(X)=z(X)z'(X) = z(X)z′(X)=z(X) for all X∈V−{A}X \in V - \{A\}X∈V−{A}. However, we still can determine the value of AAA if z(B)=bz(B) = bz(B)=b and z(C)=cz(C) = cz(C)=c for some bbb and ccc in R, in which case z′(A)=b θ cz'(A) = b\,\theta\,cz′(A)=bθc.

  • ⟨A:=r⟩(z)=z′\langle A := r \rangle(z)=z'⟨A:=r⟩(z)=z′ where z′(X)=z(X)z'(X) = z(X)z′(X)=z(X) for all X∈V−{A}X \in V - \{A\}X∈V−{A} and z′(A)=rz'(A) = rz′(A)=r.

  1. i∈Fi \in Fi∈F, where i(z)=zi(z) = zi(z)=z for all z∈Lz\in Lz∈L.
  2. if f,g∈Ff, g \in Ff,g∈F then f∘g∈Ff \circ g \in Ff∘g∈F.

Lemma 1. Let LLL be a semilattice and f1,f2,...,fnf_1, f_2, ..., f_nf1​,f2​,...,fn​ be functions on LLL. If it is true that (∀x,y∈L)(∀1≤i≤n)  [fi(x∧y)≤fi(x)∧fi(y)](\forall x, y \in L) (\forall 1 \leq i \leq n)\; [f_i(x \wedge y) \leq f_i(x) \wedge f_i(y)](∀x,y∈L)(∀1≤i≤n)[fi​(x∧y)≤fi​(x)∧fi​(y)], then f1∘f2∘...∘fn(x∧y)≤f1∘f2∘...∘fn(x)∧f1∘f2∘...∘fn(y)f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n (x \wedge y) \leq f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n (x) \wedge f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n(y)f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(x∧y)≤f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(x)∧f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(y).

proofproofproof. fn(x∧y)≤fn(x)∧fn(y)f_n(x \wedge y) \leq f_n(x) \wedge f_n(y)fn​(x∧y)≤fn​(x)∧fn​(y) (by assumption). Suppose f1∘f2∘...∘fn(x∧y)≤f1∘f2∘...∘fn(x)∧f1∘f2∘...∘fn(y)f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n (x \wedge y) \leq f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n (x) \wedge f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n(y)f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(x∧y)≤f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(x)∧f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(y), then fi−1(fi∘...∘fn(x∧y))≤fi−1(fi∘...∘fn(x))∧fi−1(fi∘...∘fn(y))f_{i-1}(f_i\circ...\circ f_n(x \wedge y)) \leq f_{i-1}(f_i\circ...\circ f_n(x)) \wedge f_{i-1}(f_i\circ...\circ f_n(y))fi−1​(fi​∘...∘fn​(x∧y))≤fi−1​(fi​∘...∘fn​(x))∧fi−1​(fi​∘...∘fn​(y)) (by Observation 1). fi−1(fi∘...∘fn(x))∧fi−1(fi∘...∘fn(y))≤fi−1∘...∘fn(x)∧fi−1∘...∘fn(y)f_{i-1}(f_i\circ...\circ f_n(x)) \wedge f_{i-1}(f_i\circ...\circ f_n(y)) \leq f_{i-1} \circ ... \circ f_n (x) \wedge f_{i-1} \circ ... \circ f_n(y)fi−1​(fi​∘...∘fn​(x))∧fi−1​(fi​∘...∘fn​(y))≤fi−1​∘...∘fn​(x)∧fi−1​∘...∘fn​(y) (by assumption). So by simple backward induction on iii, the lemma follows.

□

Theorem 1. CONST=(L,∧,F)CONST = (L, \wedge, F)CONST=(L,∧,F) is a monotone data flow analysis framework. Furthermore there exists z,z′∈Lz, z' \in Lz,z′∈L and f∈Ff \in Ff∈F such that f(z∧z′)≤f(z)∧f(z′)f(z \wedge z') \leq f(z) \wedge f(z')f(z∧z′)≤f(z)∧f(z′) .

proofproofproof. The fact that LLL is a bounded semilattice with a ⊥\bot⊥ element is obvious. Furthermore, for any element z∈L,z=f1∘f2∘...∘fn(⊥)z \in L, z = f_1 \circ f_2 \circ ... \circ f_n(\bot)z∈L,z=f1​∘f2​∘...∘fn​(⊥) for some integer nnn, where fif_ifi​ is of the form ⟨Ai=r⟩\langle A_i = r \rangle⟨Ai​=r⟩. So to show that FFF is a monotone function space associated with LLL, it suffices by Lemma 1, to show that for all z,z′∈Lz, z' \in Lz,z′∈L and all functions in FFF of the form ⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩\langle A := B \;\theta \;C \rangle⟨A:=BθC⟩ or ⟨A=r⟩\langle A = r \rangle⟨A=r⟩,

⟨A:=B θ C⟩(z∧z′)≤⟨A:=B θ C⟩(z)∧⟨A:=B θ C⟩(z′) \langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle(z \wedge z') \leq \langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle(z) \wedge \langle A := B \,\theta\, C \rangle(z') ⟨A:=BθC⟩(z∧z′)≤⟨A:=BθC⟩(z)∧⟨A:=BθC⟩(z′)

and

⟨A:=r⟩(z∧z′)≤⟨A:=r⟩(z)∧⟨A:=r⟩(z′). \langle A := r \rangle(z \wedge z' ) \leq \langle A := r \rangle(z) \wedge \langle A := r \rangle(z'). ⟨A:=r⟩(z∧z′)≤⟨A:=r⟩(z)∧⟨A:=r⟩(z′).

Observe that since ∧\wedge∧ is intersection on LLL, the ≤\leq≤ relation is set inclusion.

  1. Suppose we are given z,z′∈Lz, z' \in Lz,z′∈L and ⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩∈F\langle A := B \; \theta \; C \rangle \in F⟨A:=BθC⟩∈F. Let y=⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩(z∧z′)y = \langle A := B \; \theta \; C \rangle(z \wedge z')y=⟨A:=BθC⟩(z∧z′). Then for all X∈V−{A}X \in V - \{ A \}X∈V−{A}, if (X,r)∈y(X, r) \in y(X,r)∈y then (X,r)∈z(X, r) \in z(X,r)∈z and (X,r)∈z′(X, r) \in z'(X,r)∈z′. Hence (X,r)∈⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩(z)(X, r) \in \langle A := B \; \theta \; C \rangle(z)(X,r)∈⟨A:=BθC⟩(z) and (X,r)∈⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩(z′)(X, r) \in \langle A := B \; \theta \; C \rangle(z')(X,r)∈⟨A:=BθC⟩(z′).

    If AAA is undefined in yyy, then we are done. Suppose however, that (A,r)∈y(A, r) \in y(A,r)∈y. Then {(B,r1),(C,r2)}\{ (B, r_1), (C, r_2)\}{(B,r1​),(C,r2​)} is a subset of zzz and is also a subset of z′z'z′, for some r1r_1r1​ and r2r_2r2​ such that r=r1  θ  r2r = r_1\;\theta\;r_2r=r1​θr2​. This implies that (A,r)∈⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩(z)(A, r) \in \langle A := B\;\theta\;C\rangle(z)(A,r)∈⟨A:=BθC⟩(z) and (A,r)∈⟨A:=B  θ  C⟩(z′)(A, r) \in \langle A := B\;\theta\;C\rangle(z')(A,r)∈⟨A:=BθC⟩(z′)

  2. Suppose we are given z,z′∈Lz, z' \in Lz,z′∈L and ⟨A:=r⟩∈F\langle A := r\rangle \in F⟨A:=r⟩∈F. It is straightforward to show that ⟨A:=r⟩(z∧z′)=⟨A:=r⟩(z)∧⟨A:=r⟩(z′)\langle A := r \rangle (z \wedge z') = \langle A := r \rangle (z) \wedge \langle A := r \rangle (z')⟨A:=r⟩(z∧z′)=⟨A:=r⟩(z)∧⟨A:=r⟩(z′). Hence the first part of the lemma follows.

    For a counterexample showing that CONSTCONSTCONST is not distributive, consider the flow chart of Figure 1. There we see that ⟨C:=A+B⟩(z∧z′)=∅\langle C := A + B \rangle(z \wedge z') = \emptyset⟨C:=A+B⟩(z∧z′)=∅ , while ⟨C:=A+B⟩(z)∧⟨C:=A+B⟩(z′)={(C,5)}\langle C := A + B \rangle(z) \wedge \langle C := A + B \rangle(z') = \{ (C, 5) \}⟨C:=A+B⟩(z)∧⟨C:=A+B⟩(z′)={(C,5)} .

    □

We shall also mention that Theorem 1 can be generalized to any framework whose lattice elements associate "values" with variables, whose meet operation is intersection, and whose functions reflect the application of "operation" on those values and assignment of values to variable. The framework will be monotone in all cases, but will be distributive only if the interpretation of the operator is "free", that is, the effect of applying k-aryk\text{-}aryk-ary operator θ\thetaθ to two different k-tuplesk\text{-}tuplesk-tuples of values is never that same.

The phrase "whose functions reflect the application of 'operation' on those values and assignment of values to variable" refers to how the transfer functions in the data flow analysis framework operate on the lattice elements. Specifically, it describes the behavior of these functions in terms of:

  1. Applying operations to the values associated with variables (e.g., performing arithmetic or logical operations).
  2. Assigning new values to variables based on the results of those operations.

This means that the transfer functions (f∈F)( f \in F)(f∈F) simulate the effect of program statements on the values of variables. For example:

  • If a program statement is x = y + z, the corresponding transfer function:
    • Applies the operation +++ to the values of yyy and zzz.
    • Assigns the result to xxx.

In the lattice, this might look this:

  • If yyy is mapped to 3 and zzz is mapped to 5, the transfer function updates xxx to 8.

Approaches to Solving Monotone Data Flow Analysis Problems

It appears generally true that what one searches for in a data flow problem is what we shall call the meet  over  all  paths(MOP)meet\;over\;all\;paths (MOP)meetoverallpaths(MOP) solution. That is, let PATH(n)PATH(n)PATH(n) denote the set of paths from the initial node to node nnn in some flow graph. The we really want ⋀p∈PATH(n)fp(⊥)\bigwedge\limits_{p \in PATH(n)}f_p(\bot)p∈PATH(n)⋀​fp​(⊥) for each nnn. It is this function, the MOPMOPMOP solution that, in any practical data flow problem we can think of, expression the desired information.

The paths considered in the MOPMOPMOP solution are a superset of all the paths that are possibly executed. Thus, the MOPMOPMOP solution meets together not only the data-flow values of all the executable paths, but also additional values associated with the paths that cannot possibly by executed.

Notice that in the MOPMOPMOP solution, the number of paths considered is still unbounded if the flow graph contains cycles. Thus, the MOPMOPMOP definition does not lend itself to a direct algorithm. The iterative algorithm certainly does not first find all the paths leading to a basic block before applying the meet operator. Rather,

  1. The iterative algorithm visits basic blocks, not necessarily in the order of execution.
  2. At each confluence point, the algorithm applies the meet operator to the data-flow values obtained so far. Some of these values used were introduced artificially in the initialization process, not representing the result of any execution from the beginning of the program.

Algorithm 1 (Essentially Kildall's Algorithm applied to a monotone framework)

  Theorem 4. Given a monotone framework D=(L,∧,F)D = (L, \wedge, F)D=(L,∧,F), suppose (∃x,y∈L)(∃f∈F)[f(x∧y)<f(x)∧f(y)](\exist x, y\in L)(\exist f \in F)\left[ f(x \wedge y) < f(x) \wedge f(y) \right](∃x,y∈L)(∃f∈F)[f(x∧y)<f(x)∧f(y)], i.e. DDD is not distributive. □

In summary then, Kildall's algorithm applied to a monotone data flow analysis framework yields a unique solution, independent of the order in which nodes are visited. This solution is the maximum fixed point of the set of equations associated with a flow graph. However, we can only show that the solution is equal to or less than the MOPMOPMOP solution, and when the framework is not distributive there will always be some instance in which the inequality is strict.

A Variant of Kildall's Algorithm

This algorithm will obtain the MOPMOPMOP solution in certain situations where Algorithm 1 fails to do so. However, like Algorithm 1, it must fail for some instance of any monotone, non-distributive framework. It is interesting, however, to note that the two algorithms differ in their behavior in the general monotone case, although they are easily shown in produce identical answers for distributive frameworks.

⋀P∈PATH(n)fP(⊥)≥f(x)∧f(y)>f(x∧y)=H[n]. \bigwedge\limits_{P \in PATH(n)} f_P(\bot) \geq f(x) \wedge f(y) > f(x \wedge y) = H[n]. P∈PATH(n)⋀​fP​(⊥)≥f(x)∧f(y)>f(x∧y)=H[n].

  To prove Theorem 6 (2), we refer to Figure 2. Direct calculation will show that A[n]=f(x∧y)A[n] = f(x \wedge y)A[n]=f(x∧y). By part (3) of Theorem 5, H[mx]≥xH[m_x] \geq xH[mx​]≥x and H[my]≥yH[m_y] \geq yH[my​]≥y. Thus B[mx]≥xB[m_x] \geq xB[mx​]≥x and B[my]≥yB[m_y] \geq yB[my​]≥y. It follows that H[n]≥f(x)∧f(y)H[n] \geq f(x) \wedge f(y)H[n]≥f(x)∧f(y), so A[n]<H[n]A[n] < H[n]A[n]<H[n].

□

Undecidability of the MOP Problem for Monotone Data Flow Analysis Frameworks

We have seen that some of the obvious algorithms fail to compute the MOP solution for an arbitrary monotone framework. We shall now show that this situation must hold for any algorithm. In particular, we show that there does not exist an algorithm which, for arbitrary instance I=(G,M)I = (G, M)I=(G,M) of an arbitrary monotone data flow analysis framework D=(L,∧,F)D = (L, \wedge, F)D=(L,∧,F), will compute ⋀P∈PATH(n)fP(⊥)\bigwedge\limits_{P \in PATH(n)}f_P(\bot)P∈PATH(n)⋀​fP​(⊥) for all nodes nnn of GGG.

Definition. The Modified  Post′s  Correspondence  Problem  (MPCP)Modified \; Post's \; Correspondence \; Problem \; (MPCP)ModifiedPost′sCorrespondenceProblem(MPCP) is the following: Given arbitrary lists AAA and BBB, of kkk strings each in {0,1}+\{ 0, 1 \}^+{0,1}+, say

A=w1,w2,...,wkB=z1,z2,...,zk A = w_1, w_2, ..., w_k \qquad B = z_1, z_2, ..., z_k A=w1​,w2​,...,wk​B=z1​,z2​,...,zk​

does there exist a sequence of integers i1,i2,…,iri_1, i_2, \ldots , i_ri1​,i2​,…,ir​ such that

w1wi1wi2…wir=z1zi1zi2…zir w_1 w_{i_1} w_{i_2} \ldots w_{i_r} = z_1 z_{i_1} z_{i_2} \ldots z_{i_r} w1​wi1​​wi2​​…wir​​=z1​zi1​​zi2​​…zir​​

It is well known that MPCP is undecidable.

Example instance of MPCP. Consider the following two lists:

A={w1,w2,w3,w4}A = \{ w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4 \}A={w1​,w2​,w3​,w4​} and B={z1,z2,z3,z4}B = \{z_1, z_2, z_3, z_4\}B={z1​,z2​,z3​,z4​}

w1=010z1=010w2=0z2=100w3=01z3=00w4=110z4=11 \begin{align*} &w_1 = 010 \qquad &z_1 = 010 \\ &w_2 = 0 \qquad &z_2 = 100 \\ &w_3 = 01 \qquad &z_3 = 00 \\ &w_4 = 110 \qquad &z_4 = 11 \\ \end{align*} ​w1​=010w2​=0w3​=01w4​=110​z1​=010z2​=100z3​=00z4​=11​

A solution to this problem would be the sequence (4,3,4,2)(4, 3, 4, 2)(4,3,4,2), because

w1w4w3w4w2=010⋅110⋅01⋅110⋅0=010110011100=010⋅11⋅00⋅11⋅100=z1z4z3z4z2 w_1 w_4 w_3 w_4 w_2 = 010 \cdot 110 \cdot 01 \cdot 110 \cdot 0 = 010110011100 = 010 \cdot 11 \cdot 00 \cdot 11 \cdot 100 = z_1 z_4 z_3 z_4 z_2 w1​w4​w3​w4​w2​=010⋅110⋅01⋅110⋅0=010110011100=010⋅11⋅00⋅11⋅100=z1​z4​z3​z4​z2​

Furthermore, since (4,3,4,2)(4, 3, 4, 2)(4,3,4,2) is a solution, so are all of its "repetitions", such as (4,3,4,2,4,3,4,2)(4, 3, 4, 2, 4, 3, 4, 2)(4,3,4,2,4,3,4,2), etc.; that is, when a solution exists, there are infinitely many solutions of this repetitive kind.

Association of Post Correspondence Problem with Monotone Data Flow Analysis Frameworks

To associate PCP with monotone data flow analysis frameworks, we can think of the problem in terms of data flow through a hypothetical program. Here's how:

  1. Control Flow Graph (CFG): Construct a CFG where each node represents a step in the PCP process (e.g., selecting an index from the lists).
  2. Data Flow Information: Define data flow information that represents the partial solutions to the PCP at each node. For example, at each node, we can track the current concatenation of strings from both lists.
  3. Monotone Propagation: Propagate this data flow information through the CFG. At each node, update the data flow information based on the selected index and the current concatenation.
  4. Termination Condition: Check if the data flow information at any node matches the target concatenation. If it does, a solution to the PCP has been found.

Given an instance ABABAB of MPCP with lists A=w1,…,wkA = w_1,\ldots,w_kA=w1​,…,wk​ and B=z1,…,zkB = z_1,\ldots,z_kB=z1​,…,zk​, we can construct a monotone data flow analysis framework DAB=(LAB,∧,FAB)D_{AB} = (L_{AB}, \wedge, F_{AB})DAB​=(LAB​,∧,FAB​), where the following elements are in LABL_{AB}LAB​:

  1. ⊥\bot⊥, the lattice zero,
  2. the special element $\$$, which will in a sense indicate non-solution to MPCP, and
  3. all strings of integers 1,2,…,k1, 2, \ldots, k1,2,…,k beginning with 111.

The meet on these elements in given by: x∧y=⊥x \wedge y = \botx∧y=⊥ whenever x≠yx \neq yx=y. Thus, if x≤yx \leq yx≤y, then either x=yx = yx=y or x=⊥x = \botx=⊥.

The set of functions FABF_{AB}FAB​ includes the following.

  1. the identify function on LABL_{AB}LAB​

  2. functions fif_ifi​, for 1≤i≤k1 \leq i \leq k1≤i≤k defined by:

    1. if aaa is a string of integers beginning with 111, then fi(a)=aif_i(a) = aifi​(a)=ai,
    2. fi(⊥)=(⊥)f_i(\bot) = (\bot)fi​(⊥)=(⊥) and
    3. fi($)=$f_i(\$) = \$fi​($)=$,

  3. the function ggg defined by

    1. for string a=1i1i2…ima = 1 i_1 i_2 \ldots i_ma=1i1​i2​…im​,

    g(a)={⊥if  1i1i2…im  is a solution to instance AB of MPCP$otherwise, g(a) = \begin{cases} \bot \qquad \text{if} \; 1 i_1 i_2 \ldots i_m \; \text{is a solution to instance AB of MPCP} \\ \$ \qquad \text{otherwise}, \end{cases} g(a)={⊥if1i1​i2​…im​is a solution to instance AB of MPCP$otherwise,​

    1. g(⊥)=⊥g(\bot) = \botg(⊥)=⊥,
    2. gi($)=$g_i(\$) = \$gi​($)=$,

  4. the function hhh defined by h(x)=⊤h(x) = \toph(x)=⊤ (that is, the string consisting of ⊤\top⊤ alone) for all x∈LABx \in L_{AB}x∈LAB​ .

  5. All functions constructed from the above by composition.

Lemma 3. DAB=(LAB,∧,FAB)D_{AB} = ( L_{AB}, \wedge, F_{AB})DAB​=(LAB​,∧,FAB​) constructed above is a monotone data flow analysis framework.

ProofProofProof. We show only monotonicity; the other properties are easy to check. By Observation 1 and Lemma !, it suffices to show that if x≤yx \leq yx≤y for xxx and yyy in LABL_{AB}LAB​ then

  1. fi(x)≤fi(y)f_i(x) \leq f_i(y)fi​(x)≤fi​(y) for 1≤i≤k1 \leq i \leq k1≤i≤k ,
  2. g(x)≤g(y)g(x) \leq g(y)g(x)≤g(y), and
  3. h(x)≤h(y)h(x) \leq h(y)h(x)≤h(y).

Since h(x)=h(y)=⊤h(x) = h(y) = \toph(x)=h(y)=⊤, (3) is immediate. We have observed that for the meet operation we have defined, x≤yx \leq yx≤y implies xxx is either yyy or ⊥\bot⊥. In the former case (1) and (2) are immediate.

In the latter case, fi(x)=⊥f_i(x) = \botfi​(x)=⊥ and g(x)=⊥g(x) = \botg(x)=⊥, so fi(x)≤fi(y)f_i(x) \leq f_i(y)fi​(x)≤fi​(y) and g(x)=g(y)g(x) = g(y)g(x)=g(y) follows.

Theorem 7. There does not exist an algorithm AAA with the following properties.

  1. the input to AAA is
    1. Algorithms to perform meet, equality testing and application of functions to lattice elements for some monotone framework and,
    2. An instance III of the framework.
  2. The output of AAA is the MOPMOPMOP solution for I.

ProofProofProof. If AAA exists, then we can apply AAA to any monotone framework DABD_{AB}DAB​ constructed above with the instance III. The MOP solution to that problem at point ppp is easily seen to be $\$$ if the instance ABABAB of the MPCP has no solution, and ⊥\bot⊥ if it does. Thus, if AAA existed, we could solve the MPCP.

We can construct a CFG where each node represents selecting a index from MPCP lists, and data flow information represents the partial solutions to the MPCP at each node. If the data flow information obtained from different paths is different (which means there is no solution to this MPCP) , then we always obtain ⊥\bot⊥ by definition of meet operator (meet on these elements in given by: x∧y=⊥x \wedge y = \botx∧y=⊥ whenever x≠yx \neq yx=y. Thus, if x≤yx \leq yx≤y, then either x=yx = yx=y or x=⊥x = \botx=⊥.). By definition of function ggg, therefore we obtain $\$$, which will in a sense indicate non-solution to MPCP.

References

  1. Kam, J.B. and Jeffrey D. Ullman. Monotone Data Flow Analysis Frameworks, Tech. Rept. No. 169, Dept, of Elec. Engg., Princeton Univ., Princeton, NJ, 1975.
  2. Kildall, Gary A. A Unified Approach to Global Program Optimization, in [POPL73], pp. 194-206.
  3. Wikipedia - Post correspondence problem
  4. Muchnick and Steven S. Advanced compiler design and implementation.
  5. Aho, Alfred V. Compilers: principles, techniques, & tools.

item.title: Static Single Assignment Form
item.description: Static Single Assignment Form In optimizing compilers, data structure choices directly influence the power and efficiency of practical program optimization. A poor choice of dat...
item.content:encoded:
In optimizing compilers, data structure choices directly influence the power and efficiency of practical program optimization. A poor choice of data structure can inhibit optimization or slow compilation to the point where advanced optimization features become undesirable. Static single-assignment form is a relatively new intermediate representation that effectively separates the values operated on in a program from the locations they are stored in, making possible more effective versions of several optimizations.

The translation process into SSA form first figures out at what join points to insert ϕ\phiϕ-functions, then inserts trivial ϕ\phiϕ-functions (i.e., ϕ\phiϕ-functions of the form ϕ(x,x,…,x)\phi (x, x , \ldots , x )ϕ(x,x,…,x)) with the number of argument positions equal to the number of control-flow predecessors of the join point that some definition of the variable reaches, and then renames definitions and uses of variables (conventionally by subscripting them) to establish the static single-assignment property. Once we have finished doing whatever we translated to SSA form for, we need to eliminate the ϕ\phiϕ-functions, since they are only a conceptual tool, and are not computationally effective—i.e., when we come to a join point with a ϕ\phiϕ-function while executing a procedure, we have no way to determine which branch we came to it by and hence which value to use.

Where to Place ϕ\phiϕ-Functions

At first glance, careful placement might seem to require the enumeration of pairs of assignment statements for each variable. Checking whether there are two assignments to V that reach a common point might seem to be intrinsically nonlinear. In fact, however, it is enough to look at the dominance frontier of each node in the control flow graph. Leaving the technicalities to later sections, we sketch the method here.

Suppose that a variable VVV has just one assignment in the original program, so that any use of VVV will be either a use of V0V_0V0​ at entry to the program or a use of V1V_1V1​ from the most recent execution of the assignment to VVV. Let XXX be the basic block of code that assigns to VVV, so XXX will determine the value of VVV when control flows along any edge X→YX \rarr YX→Y to a basic block YYY. When entered along X→YX \rarr YX→Y, the code in YYY will see V1V_1V1​ and be unaffected by V0V_0V0​. If Y≠XY \neq XY=X, but all paths to YYY must still go through XXX (in which case XXX is said to strictly  dominate  Ystrictly \; dominate \; YstrictlydominateY), then the code in YYY will always see V1V_1V1​. Indeed, any node strictly dominated by XXX will always see V1V_1V1​, no matter how far from XXX it may be. Eventually, however, control may be able to reach a node ZZZ not strictly dominated by XXX. Suppose ZZZ is the first such node on a path, so that ZZZ sees V1V_1V1​ along one in-edge but may see V0V_0V0​ along another in-edge. Then ZZZ is said to be in  the  dominance  frontierin \; the \; dominance \; frontierinthedominancefrontier of XXX and is clearly in need of a ϕ\phiϕ-function for VVV. In general, no matter how many assignments to VVV may appear in the original and no matter how complex the control flow may be, we can place ϕ\phiϕ-functions for VVV by finding the dominance frontier of every node that assigns to VVV, then the dominance frontier of every node where a ϕ\phiϕ-function has already been placed, and so on.

Dominance Frontier

For a flowgraph node xxx, the dominance frontier of xxx, written DF(x)DF(x)DF(x), is the set of all nodes yyy in the flowgraph such that xxx dominates an immediate predecessor of yyy but does not strictly dominate yyy, i.e.,

DF(x)={y  ∣(∃z∈Pred(y)  such that  x  dom  z)  and  x  !sdom  y} DF(x) = \left\{ y \; | (\exist z \in Pred(y) \; \text{such that}\; x \; dom \; z ) \; \text{and} \; x \; !sdom \; y\right\} DF(x)={y∣(∃z∈Pred(y)such thatxdomz)andx!sdomy}

Computing DF(x)DF(x)DF(x) directly for all xxx would be quadratic in the number of nodes in the flowgraph. An algorithm that is linear results from breaking it into the computation of two intermediate components, DFlocal(x)DF_{local}(x)DFlocal​(x) and DFup(x,z)DF_{up}(x, z)DFup​(x,z), as follows:

DFlocal(x)={y∈Succ(x)  ∣  idom(y)≠x}DFup(x,z)={y∈DF(z)  ∣  idom(z)=x  &  idom(y)≠x} DF_{local}(x) = \{ y \in Succ(x) \; | \; idom(y) \neq x \} \\ DF_{up}(x, z) = \{ y \in DF(z) \; | \; idom(z) = x \; \& \; idom(y) \neq x \} DFlocal​(x)={y∈Succ(x)∣idom(y)=x}DFup​(x,z)={y∈DF(z)∣idom(z)=x&idom(y)=x}

and computing DF(x)DF(x)DF(x) as

DF(x)=DFlocal(x)⋃z∈N(idom(z)=x)DFup(x,z) DF(x) = DF_{local}(x) \bigcup\limits_{z \in N(idom(z) = x)} DF_{up} (x, z) DF(x)=DFlocal​(x)z∈N(idom(z)=x)⋃​DFup​(x,z)

To compute the dominance frontier for a given flowgraph, we turn the above equations into the code shown in bellow snippet.

Iterated Dominance Frontier(DF+DF^+DF+)

Now, we define for a set of flowgraph nodes SSS, the dominance frontier of SSS as

DF(S)=⋃x∈SDF(x) DF(S) = \bigcup\limits_{x \in S} DF(x) DF(S)=x∈S⋃​DF(x)

and the iterated dominance frontier DF+()DF^+()DF+() as

DF+(S)=lim⁡i→∞DFi(S) DF^+(S) = \lim\limits_{i \rarr \infty} DF^i(S) DF+(S)=i→∞lim​DFi(S)

where DF1(S)=DF(S)DF^1(S) = DF(S)DF1(S)=DF(S) and DFi+1(S)=DF(S∪DFi(S))DF^{i+1}(S) = DF(S \cup DF^i(S))DFi+1(S)=DF(S∪DFi(S)). If SSS is the set of nodes that assign to variable xxx, plus the entry node, then DF+(S)DF^+(S)DF+(S) is exactly the set of nodes that need ϕ\phiϕ-functions for xxx.

Key Properties

  1. SSA Construction:

    Used to determine where ϕ\phiϕ-functions are needed. If a variable is defined in SSS, ϕ−\phi-ϕ−functions are placed at DF+(S)DF^+(S)DF+(S).

  2. Convergence Points:

    Nodes in DF(B)DF(B)DF(B) are points where control flow paths merge, ending B′sB'sB′s dominance.

References

  1. RON CYTRON, JEANNE FERRANTE, BARRY K. ROSEN, and MARK N. WEGMAN IBM Research Division and F. KENNETH ZADECK Brown University. Efficiently computing static single assignment form and the control dependence graph. ACM Trans. Program. Lang. Syst. p451-490. 10/1991. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/115372.115320
  2. Muchnick and Steven S. Advanced compiler design and implementation.
